questions.stem: The first-order necessary condition for $x^*$ to be a local optimum of a differentiable function $f$ on an open set is
questions.explanation: At an interior optimum the gradient must vanish, $\nabla f(x^*) = 0$, since otherwise moving along $-\nabla f$ would decrease $f$. A positive Hessian is a second-order condition, and $f(x^*) = 0$ concerns the value not the location, so the vanishing gradient is the first-order condition.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: A point where $\nabla f = 0$ is called a
questions.explanation: A point at which the gradient is zero is a stationary point, also called a critical point, and is a candidate for a local optimum. It need not be a boundary, inflection, or merely feasible point, so stationary point is the correct name.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: For a single-variable function, if $f'(x^*) = 0$ and $f''(x^*) > 0$, then $x^*$ is a
questions.explanation: A vanishing first derivative with a positive second derivative means the curve is locally bowl-shaped, so $x^*$ is a local minimum. A negative second derivative would give a maximum, so the positive case gives a minimum.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: For a single-variable function, if $f'(x^*) = 0$ and $f''(x^*) < 0$, then $x^*$ is a
questions.explanation: A zero first derivative with a negative second derivative indicates a locally dome-shaped curve, so $x^*$ is a local maximum. The positive case would instead give a minimum, so the negative second derivative yields a maximum.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: For a function of two variables at a stationary point, with Hessian determinant $D = f_{xx}f_{yy} - f_{xy}^2$, the point is a saddle point when
questions.explanation: A negative Hessian determinant $D < 0$ means the Hessian is indefinite, so the surface rises in one direction and falls in another, a saddle. The cases $D > 0$ with $f_{xx} > 0$ or $f_{xx} < 0$ give a minimum or maximum, while $D = 0$ is inconclusive, so $D < 0$ marks the saddle.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: At a stationary point of $f(x, y)$, if $D = f_{xx}f_{yy} - f_{xy}^2 > 0$ and $f_{xx} > 0$, the point is a
questions.explanation: When $D > 0$ the Hessian is definite, and a positive $f_{xx}$ makes it positive definite, indicating a local minimum. A negative $f_{xx}$ would instead give a maximum, so the positive case yields a minimum.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: Find the stationary point of $f(x, y) = x^2 + y^2 - 4x - 6y + 13$.
questions.explanation: Setting $f_x = 2x - 4 = 0$ gives $x = 2$, and $f_y = 2y - 6 = 0$ gives $y = 3$. So the only stationary point is $(2, 3)$, where the gradient vanishes.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: Continuing with $f(x, y) = x^2 + y^2 - 4x - 6y + 13$ at its stationary point $(2, 3)$, the Hessian is $\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$, so the point is a
questions.explanation: The Hessian determinant is $D = (2)(2) - 0 = 4 > 0$ and $f_{xx} = 2 > 0$, so the Hessian is positive definite and $(2, 3)$ is a local minimum. Since $f$ is convex, it is in fact the global minimum, with value $f(2, 3) = 0$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: Classify the stationary point $(0, 0)$ of $f(x, y) = x^2 - y^2$, whose Hessian is $\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 \end{pmatrix}$.
questions.explanation: The Hessian determinant is $D = (2)(-2) - 0 = -4 < 0$, so the Hessian is indefinite and the point is a saddle. Indeed $f$ increases along the $x$-axis and decreases along the $y$-axis, the signature of a saddle.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: Find all stationary points of the single-variable function $f(x) = x^3 - 3x$.
questions.explanation: Setting $f'(x) = 3x^2 - 3 = 0$ gives $x^2 = 1$, so $x = 1$ and $x = -1$. These are the two stationary points of the cubic.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: For $f(x) = x^3 - 3x$ with $f''(x) = 6x$, classify the stationary points $x = 1$ and $x = -1$.
options: $x = 1$ is a maximum, $x = -1$ is a minimum
$x = 1$ is a minimum, $x = -1$ is a maximum
questions.explanation: At $x = 1$, $f''(1) = 6 > 0$, so it is a local minimum, and at $x = -1$, $f''(-1) = -6 < 0$, so it is a local maximum. The sign of $f''$ at each critical point decides its type.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: Find the stationary point of $f(x, y) = -(x^2 + y^2) + 2x + 4y$.
questions.explanation: Setting $f_x = -2x + 2 = 0$ gives $x = 1$, and $f_y = -2y + 4 = 0$ gives $y = 2$. So the stationary point is $(1, 2)$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: At the stationary point $(1, 2)$ of $f(x, y) = -(x^2 + y^2) + 2x + 4y$, the Hessian is $\begin{pmatrix} -2 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 \end{pmatrix}$. The point is a
questions.explanation: The determinant is $D = (-2)(-2) - 0 = 4 > 0$ with $f_{xx} = -2 < 0$, so the Hessian is negative definite and the point is a local maximum. The maximum value is $f(1, 2) = -(1 + 4) + 2 + 8 = 5$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: Find and classify the stationary point of $f(x, y) = x^2 + y^2 - 2x - 4y + 5$.
questions.explanation: From $f_x = 2x - 2 = 0$ and $f_y = 2y - 4 = 0$ the stationary point is $(1, 2)$. The Hessian is $\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$ with $D = 4 > 0$ and $f_{xx} > 0$, so it is a local minimum with value $f(1, 2) = 0$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: Find the stationary point of $f(x, y) = x^2 + xy + y^2 - 3x$.
questions.explanation: The equations $f_x = 2x + y - 3 = 0$ and $f_y = x + 2y = 0$ give $x = -2y$; substituting yields $-4y + y - 3 = 0$, so $y = -1$ and $x = 2$. The stationary point is $(2, -1)$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: At the stationary point $(2, -1)$ of $f(x, y) = x^2 + xy + y^2 - 3x$, the Hessian is $\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$. The point is a
questions.explanation: The determinant is $D = (2)(2) - (1)(1) = 3 > 0$ and $f_{xx} = 2 > 0$, so the Hessian is positive definite and the point is a local minimum. Its value is $f(2, -1) = 4 - 2 + 1 - 6 = -3$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: Find and classify the stationary point of $f(x, y) = 2x^2 + 2y^2 - 2xy - 6x$.
questions.explanation: From $f_x = 4x - 2y - 6 = 0$ and $f_y = 4y - 2x = 0$ we get $x = 2y$, then $8y - 2y - 6 = 0$ gives $y = 1$ and $x = 2$. The Hessian $\begin{pmatrix} 4 & -2 \\ -2 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$ has $D = 12 > 0$ and $f_{xx} > 0$, so $(2, 1)$ is a local minimum with value $-6$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: When the Hessian determinant $D = 0$ at a stationary point of $f(x, y)$, the second-order test
questions.explanation: A zero determinant means the Hessian is only semidefinite, so it cannot distinguish a minimum, maximum, or degenerate behaviour. The test is therefore inconclusive and higher-order analysis is needed.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: The function $f(x, y) = x^3 - 3xy + y^3$ has stationary points where $f_x = 3x^2 - 3y = 0$ and $f_y = -3x + 3y^2 = 0$. These points are
questions.explanation: From the first equation $y = x^2$, and substituting into the second gives $-3x + 3x^4 = 0$, so $x(x^3 - 1) = 0$ and $x = 0$ or $x = 1$. The corresponding points are $(0, 0)$ and $(1, 1)$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: For $f(x, y) = x^3 - 3xy + y^3$ with Hessian $\begin{pmatrix} 6x & -3 \\ -3 & 6y \end{pmatrix}$, classify the two stationary points $(0, 0)$ and $(1, 1)$.
options: $(0, 0)$ is a saddle, $(1, 1)$ is a local minimum
$(0, 0)$ is a local minimum, $(1, 1)$ is a saddle
questions.explanation: At $(0, 0)$ the Hessian is $\begin{pmatrix} 0 & -3 \\ -3 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$ with $D = -9 < 0$, a saddle. At $(1, 1)$ it is $\begin{pmatrix} 6 & -3 \\ -3 & 6 \end{pmatrix}$ with $D = 27 > 0$ and $f_{xx} = 6 > 0$, a local minimum with value $f(1, 1) = -1$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: Find the global minimum value of $f(x, y) = x^2 - 6x + y^2 - 8y$ over $\mathbb{R}^2$.
questions.explanation: Setting $f_x = 2x - 6 = 0$ and $f_y = 2y - 8 = 0$ gives the minimizer $(3, 4)$. Since the Hessian is $\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$ (positive definite), this is the global minimum, with value $9 - 18 + 16 - 32 = -25$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: Find the stationary points of $f(x) = x^3 - 6x^2 + 9x + 1$.
questions.explanation: The derivative is $f'(x) = 3x^2 - 12x + 9 = 3(x - 1)(x - 3)$, which is zero at $x = 1$ and $x = 3$. These are the two stationary points of the cubic.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: For $f(x) = x^3 - 6x^2 + 9x + 1$ with $f''(x) = 6x - 12$, the nature of $x = 1$ and $x = 3$ is
questions.explanation: At $x = 1$, $f''(1) = -6 < 0$, so it is a local maximum (value $5$), and at $x = 3$, $f''(3) = 6 > 0$, so it is a local minimum (value $1$). The second derivative changes sign between the two critical points.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: The gradient of $f(x, y) = x^2 + 3y^2$ is
questions.explanation: Differentiating gives $f_x = 2x$ and $f_y = 6y$, so $\nabla f = (2x, 6y)$. Setting both components to zero yields the stationary point $(0, 0)$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: Find and classify the stationary point of $f(x, y) = x^2 + y^2 - 4x - 6y + 13$.
questions.solution: Setting $f_x = 2x - 4 = 0$ and $f_y = 2y - 6 = 0$ gives the stationary point $(x, y) = ($ {{1}} $, $ {{2}} $)$. The Hessian determinant is $D = 4$, and since $f_{xx} > 0$, the point is a local {{3}}.
questions.explanation: The gradient vanishes at $(2, 3)$. With $D = (2)(2) - 0 = 4 > 0$ and $f_{xx} = 2 > 0$, the Hessian is positive definite, so the point is a local minimum.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: Find and classify the stationary point of $f(x, y) = -(x^2 + y^2) + 2x + 4y$.
questions.solution: From $f_x = -2x + 2 = 0$ and $f_y = -2y + 4 = 0$, the stationary point is $(x, y) = ($ {{1}} $, $ {{2}} $)$. The Hessian determinant is $4$ with $f_{xx} < 0$, so the point is a local {{3}}, where $f$ takes the value {{4}}.
questions.explanation: The gradient vanishes at $(1, 2)$. With $D = 4 > 0$ and $f_{xx} = -2 < 0$ the Hessian is negative definite, so the point is a local maximum with value $f(1, 2) = -(1 + 4) + 2 + 8 = 5$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: Classify the stationary point $(0, 0)$ of $f(x, y) = x^2 - y^2$.
questions.solution: The Hessian is $\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 \end{pmatrix}$, so the determinant is $D =$ {{1}}. Because $D$ is negative, the second-order test classifies $(0, 0)$ as a {{2}}.
questions.explanation: The determinant is $D = (2)(-2) - 0 = -4 < 0$, so the Hessian is indefinite. A negative determinant at a stationary point indicates a saddle point.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: Find and classify the stationary points of $f(x) = x^3 - 3x$, using $f''(x) = 6x$.
questions.solution: Solving $f'(x) = 3x^2 - 3 = 0$ gives $x =$ {{1}} and $x = -1$ (enter the positive root). Since $f''$ at the positive root is positive, that point is a local {{2}}, while $x = -1$ is a local {{3}}.
questions.explanation: The critical points are $x = 1$ and $x = -1$. Since $f''(1) = 6 > 0$ the point $x = 1$ is a local minimum, and since $f''(-1) = -6 < 0$ the point $x = -1$ is a local maximum.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: Find and classify the stationary point of $f(x, y) = x^2 + xy + y^2 - 3x$.
questions.solution: From $f_x = 2x + y - 3 = 0$ and $f_y = x + 2y = 0$, the stationary point is $(x, y) = ($ {{1}} $, $ {{2}} $)$. The Hessian $\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$ has determinant $D =$ {{3}}, so with $f_{xx} > 0$ the point is a local minimum.
questions.explanation: Solving the linear system gives $x = 2$, $y = -1$. The Hessian determinant is $D = (2)(2) - (1)(1) = 3 > 0$ with $f_{xx} = 2 > 0$, so the point is a local minimum.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: Find the global minimum of $f(x, y) = x^2 - 6x + y^2 - 8y$ on $\mathbb{R}^2$.
questions.solution: Setting the gradient to zero gives the minimizer $(x, y) = ($ {{1}} $, $ {{2}} $)$. Substituting back, the minimum value of $f$ is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: From $2x - 6 = 0$ and $2y - 8 = 0$ the minimizer is $(3, 4)$. Then $f(3, 4) = 9 - 18 + 16 - 32 = -25$, the global minimum since the Hessian is positive definite.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)
